---
title: "Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts"
chapter_id: "coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "coil_attention_memory"
  - "circle_ai_contract_suite"
  - "theseus_circle_transfer"
  - "vcm_public"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `coil_attention_memory`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer`, `vcm_public` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `coil_attention_memory`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`; supporting: `theseus_circle_transfer`, `vcm_public`, `verification_bandwidth` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `vcm_public`; raw cache: `vcm_public`; pending source notes: `coil_attention_memory`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Memory, attention, and recurrence mechanisms need finite structural contracts for aliasing, coverage, freshness, active work, loop exits, and overthinking boundaries.

Within the ASI Stack, this is a capability substrate layer problem. The layer receives tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts and must turn them into routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Circle Calculus and Proof-Carrying AI Contracts, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to CoilRA, MultiCoil RoPE, and Cyclic Mixers, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Long context, sparse attention, ring buffers, and recursive loops can silently hide stale reads, uncovered lags, duplicate slots, alias collisions, or unbounded work.

The common failure is to treat fluent output as if it already contained the missing system contract. In practice, capability growth can become a hidden monolith if routing, compression, representation, and residual accounting are not explicit. A living ASI architecture needs the opposite discipline: every layer should say what it may read, what it may decide, what it may cause, what evidence it owes, and what must happen when its conditions fail.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Coil attention and memory contracts should be used as structural guardrails for cyclic memory, sparse coverage, recurrence schedules, and work-budget admission, not as claims of retrieval or reasoning quality.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Coil Attention and Memory, Circle Calculus AI Contract Suite, Theseus Circle Calculus Transfer Lane, and Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, map memory slots, residue, winding, strides, windows, and recurrence schedules into finite circular structures. Second, expose alias, freshness, coverage, active-token, work-saving, and loop-exit fields as auditable contract outputs. Third, compare slot-only, slot-plus-winding, FIFO, LRU, content-gated, and ordinary attention or memory baselines before promoting performance claims.

- Map memory slots, residue, winding, strides, windows, and recurrence schedules into finite circular structures.
- Expose alias, freshness, coverage, active-token, work-saving, and loop-exit fields as auditable contract outputs.
- Compare slot-only, slot-plus-winding, FIFO, LRU, content-gated, and ordinary attention or memory baselines before promoting performance claims.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

Interfaces are the main protection against anthology drift. They force each idea to declare what it gives to the rest of the stack and what it refuses to decide.

- VCM context traces expose context adequacy and authority labels.
- Attention and memory modules consume contract fields as diagnostic guardrails.
- Benchmark adapters and Theseus transfer lanes decide whether structural facts survive actual workloads.
- Proof contracts define the finite boundary of the claim.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts. The downstream interface emits routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Residue-only aliasing remains visible.
- Full structural coverage is not semantic coverage.
- Recurrence budgets include exits and guardrails.
- Stale reads fail closed or enter residual escrow.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Alias hiding.
- Overthinking or non-terminating recurrence loops.
- Uncovered sparse-attention lags.
- Freshness facts treated as retrieval-quality evidence.
- No ordinary baseline controls.

The mitigation pattern is to convert each failure into an explicit artifact: a rejected handoff, a residual record, a stale-context marker, a failed proof obligation, a benchmark regression, or a human-review queue. The stack should prefer visible incompleteness over invisible confidence.

## Minimal implementation

A contract-backed memory and attention evaluation plan with slot, winding, coverage, freshness, active-token, loop-exit, work-budget, and baseline fields.

For a first implementation, the goal is not to build a full agent around the chapter. The goal is to create the smallest public-safe artifact that can be validated in CI, rendered in the book, and inspected by a future writing or coding run. That artifact can be a JSON schema, a Lean predicate, a deterministic test fixture, a trace table, a source-note map, or a replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Cyclic alias visibility test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts boundary. | planned; not run |
| Sparse coverage gap test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts boundary. | planned; not run |
| Recurrence budget and exit test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts boundary. | planned; not run |
| Freshness stale-read rejection test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:coil_memory.alias_boundary.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.CoilAttentionMemory` | A cyclic memory claim records residue and winding or marks aliasing as visible residual risk. | planned |
| `lean:coil_attention.coverage_not_quality.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.CoilAttentionMemory` | Sparse coverage or freshness facts alone cannot promote a retrieval-quality claim. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `coil_attention_memory` | Coil Attention and Memory | cyclic_attention_memory | Proof-linked cyclic memory, KV-cache freshness, sparse-attention coverage, recurrence schedules, loop-exit certificates, work budgets, and alias diagnostics. | source note pending |
| `circle_ai_contract_suite` | Circle Calculus AI Contract Suite | proof_carrying_ai_contracts | Theorem-linked AI contract families for RoPE, KV-cache freshness, sparse attention, recurrence schedules, strided fanout, cyclic memory, multicoil phase, cyclic mixers, and seed-rule regeneration. | source note pending |
| `theseus_circle_transfer` | Theseus Circle Calculus Transfer Lane | proof_contract_transfer | Report-only bridge from Circle finite fixtures into private Theseus benchmark design with explicit quality/runtime/memory/transfer/failure-case claim boundaries. | source note pending |
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Coil attention and memory contracts should be used as structural guardrails for cyclic memory, sparse coverage, recurrence schedules, and work-budget admission, not as claims of retrieval or reasoning quality. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

The chapter's v0.2 contribution is a complete architectural pass: it names the problem, states the mechanism, exposes interfaces and invariants, records failure modes, identifies minimal implementation work, and keeps source and proof obligations visible. The v1.0 work is to mine the remaining sources, promote only the claims that earn promotion, and replace planned tests with executed artifacts.
